planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; Moon and Stars is one of the most iconic American heirloom watermelons, nearly lost by the 1970s and rescued by Seed Savers Exchange founder Kent Whealy in 1980 from Missouri farmer Merle Van Doren — producing large, dramatically patterned fruits with one large yellow spot (the moon) and scattered small spots (the stars) on dark green skin.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep in well-amended, well-drained soil after last frost; thin or transplant to 36–72 inches apart in rows 6–8 feet apart. Moon and Stars is one of America's most celebrated heirloom watermelons, named for the distinctive yellow spots that cover the dark green skin — typically one large spot ('the moon') surrounded by many smaller spots ('the stars'); some strains also have similarly spotted leaves and stems. By the 1970s the variety was essentially lost from commerce and was believed extinct until Kent Whealy of Seed Savers Exchange tracked down Missouri farmer Merle Van Doren as the last known grower in 1980 and began redistribution. The large, oblong fruits weigh 20–40 lbs and produce sweet, red (or in some strains, yellow) flesh with excellent rich flavor. Days to maturity are 85–100, making it better suited to long-season gardens in zones 6–11; in shorter-season areas, start indoors 3–4 weeks before transplanting.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 70°F; Black Diamond was the dominant commercial and roadside-stand watermelon of the American South in the 1950s and 1960s, producing very large, nearly spherical fruits with dramatically dark blue-green skin and exceptionally sweet red flesh — a classic variety now prized by home gardeners and heirloom melon enthusiasts.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep in well-drained, fertile, warm soil after last frost; Black Diamond needs full sun and warm nights — it performs best in zones 7–11 and Gulf Coast gardens where the long, hot summers allow the large fruits to fully develop. Black Diamond was one of the most important commercial watermelon varieties of mid-20th century America, grown throughout the South for roadside stands, truck farming, and local markets; the very large (30–50+ lbs), round to oblong fruits have an unusually dark, blue-green skin that approaches black in color and firm, bright red flesh with excellent, classic sweet watermelon flavor. The tough skin and firm flesh gave Black Diamond reasonable shipping quality in an era before refrigerated transport was universal. Now prized as an heirloom variety for gardeners wanting the authentic large-fruited Southern watermelon experience. Days to maturity of 85–90 make it best for long-season Southern and Gulf Coast gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, well-prepared, well-drained soil; Congo is a classic Southern 'picnic' watermelon producing enormous oblong fruits (35–50+ lbs) with dark green skin and excellent deep red flesh — one of the largest-fruited traditional Southern watermelon varieties and a staple of Gulf Coast summer gardens and roadside stands.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost in full sun; Congo requires plenty of space (hills or rows 6–8 feet apart) for its vigorous, spreading vines and very large fruits. Congo is a classic Southern summer watermelon of the 'picnic' or 'family' type, producing very large, elongated-oblong fruits (35–50+ lbs) with dark green skin patterned with lighter green stripes and solid deep red flesh with excellent sweetness and rich melon flavor; Congo-type watermelons were an important part of Gulf Coast and Deep South truck farming and summer culture in the first half of the 20th century, when enormous watermelons were hauled to market and sold by the slice from wagons and stands. The very large size makes Congo impractical for modern small households (a single fruit can feed 30 people) but it remains grown by enthusiasts of traditional Southern garden culture and heirloom melon growers. Best suited to long-season Southern gardens (zones 7–11) where 85–95 day maturity can be fully achieved.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Klondike Striped is an early 20th century California heirloom watermelon that became the commercial standard for Western US watermelon production, producing medium-large, uniformly striped, oblong fruits with firm, excellent-quality red flesh.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost in full sun; Klondike Striped is drought-tolerant once established and performs well in hot, dry Western climates and Gulf Coast sandy soils. Klondike Striped was developed in early 20th century California, where it became the primary commercial watermelon for California's San Joaquin Valley and other Western growing regions; the medium-large (25–35 lbs), oblong fruits have attractive, uniform, alternating light and dark green stripes and firm, deep red to scarlet flesh with excellent, rich, sweet flavor and few seeds relative to its size. The variety became widely grown in the West and South for its reliable production, attractive appearance, and good quality; the firm flesh holds well after cutting, making it a practical variety for families and market growers. Days to maturity of 80–90 suit it well for both long-season Southern and moderately short Pacific Coast growing seasons.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Sweet Siberian is one of the shortest-season watermelons available, maturing in 65–75 days, with more cold-tolerant germination than most varieties — making it the top choice for zone 3–5 gardens with short summers where most watermelons cannot ripen, while still producing sweet, flavorful fruit.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost in full sun; Sweet Siberian can germinate in soils as cool as 60°F (compared to 70°F+ needed by most watermelons), making early establishment easier in northern gardens. Sweet Siberian is a Russian heirloom watermelon developed for the short, cool growing seasons of Siberia and Central Russia, where conventional watermelon varieties fail to ripen before fall frost; it is one of the most cold-tolerant watermelons for germination and early growth while maturing in 65–75 days from seed — roughly 15–25 days earlier than most mainstream varieties. The small to medium fruits (8–15 lbs) have pale green, lightly striped skin and unusual salmon-orange to pale yellow flesh that is surprisingly sweet and fragrant with a distinctive aroma different from standard red-fleshed varieties. Compact vines suit smaller gardens. An outstanding variety for northern zone 3–5 gardeners who have given up on watermelons, and produces a fast summer crop in Gulf Coast and Southern gardens as well.
